claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 17 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 18 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 17 and in approved education
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 18 and not in approved education or extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 17 and in approved education; Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is 17 and in approved education
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Blake
expected_eligibility.reason: Child is 18 and not in approved education or extension period

Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit.

To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you a few questions about your circumstances.

First, let me ask: Do you have any children that you're responsible for?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I do. I have two children - Alex, who's 17, and Blake, who's 18.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! I'll help you assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex and Blake.

Let me start by asking: Is Alex still in approved education or training?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex is still in sixth form doing A-levels.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That's helpful to know. Now, regarding Blake who is 18 - is Blake still in approved education or training (such as A-levels, Scottish Highers, or similar)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Blake finished school and isn't in any education or training at the moment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. Now I need to ask a few more questions about your circumstances.

Do you live in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - in Manchester.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great, thank you. Now, are both Alex and Blake living with you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, both of them live at home with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. Just a couple more questions to complete the assessment.

Are you responsible for Alex and Blake? (This means you're responsible for things like their care, food, and clothing.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, absolutely. I'm responsible for their care, food, clothing, and everything they need.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect. One final question: Is anyone else claiming Child Benefit for either Alex or Blake?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for them.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for providing all that information. Let me read the official guidance to confirm the eligibility rules before giving you my assessment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Now I have all the information I need to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for both Alex and Blake.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Based on my assessment of your circumstances against the official Child Benefit eligibility rules, here's the outcome:

**For Alex (17):** ✓ **You ARE eligible for Child Benefit**
Alex is under 20 and still in approved education (sixth form doing A-levels). Since you live in the UK, Alex lives with you, you're responsible for their care, and nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for them, you meet all the eligibility criteria.

**For Blake (18):** ✗ **You are NOT eligible for Child Benefit**
Although Blake is under 20 and lives with you, Child Benefit for young people aged 16 or over only continues if they stay in approved education or training. Since Blake has finished school and is not currently in any education or training, they don't meet the eligibility criteria.

**Important note:** If Blake decides to return to education or training in the future, or if they register with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services within their first 20 weeks after leaving education (if they were 16 or 17 when they left), you may be able to claim Child Benefit again for them during that time.

Would you like any clarification about this assessment?

reasonings: Alex is 17 years old and still in approved education (sixth form doing A-levels). According to the eligibility rules, you normally qualify for Child Benefit for a child under 16, but you'll still qualify if your child is under 20 and they stay in approved education or training. Alex meets this criteria as they are under 20 and in approved education. You live in the UK, Alex lives with you, you are responsible for their care, and nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. Therefore, you are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

Blake is 18 years old and has finished school and is not in any education or training. According to the eligibility rules, Child Benefit for children aged 16 or over only continues if the child is under 20 AND stays in approved education or training. Since Blake is not in approved education or training, they do not meet the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit, even though they are under 20 and living with you. Therefore, you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Blake.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant lives in the UK with two children: Alex (17) and Blake (18). The claimant is responsible for both children and nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for them. Alex is eligible because they are under 20 and still in approved education (sixth form A-levels). Blake is not eligible because, although under 20, they have finished education and are not in any approved education or training, which is a requirement for Child Benefit for young people aged 16 or over.

Alex meets this criteria as they are under 20 and in approved education. You live in the UK, Alex lives with you, you are responsible for their care, and nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. Therefore, you are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

Blake is 18 years old and has finished school and is not in any education or training. According to the eligibility rules, Child Benefit for children aged 16 or over only continues if the child is under 20 AND stays in approved education or training. Since Blake is not in approved education or training, they do not meet the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit, even though they are under 20 and living with you. Therefore, you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Blake.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant lives in the UK with two children: Alex (17) and Blake (18). The claimant is responsible for both children and nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for them. Alex is eligible because they are under 20 and still in approved education (sixth form A-levels). Blake is not eligible because, although under 20, they have finished education and are not in any approved education or training, which is a requirement for Child Benefit for young people aged 16 or over.
